questions.question:
Consider the following pseudo code. What is the total number of multiplications to be performed?

D = 2
for i = 1 to n do
  for j = i to n do
    for k = j + 1 to n do
      D = D * 3

A. Half of the product of the 3 consecutive integers.
B. One-third of the product of the 3 consecutive integers.
C. One-sixth of the product of the 3 consecutive integers.
D. None of the above.
questions.answer: C. One-sixth of the product of the 3 consecutive integers.
questions.to_find: Total number of multiplications performed by the triple nested loop.

understand.plain: We have a triple nested loop where i goes from 1 to n, j goes from i to n, and k goes from j+1 to n. Each time the innermost body executes, one multiplication D = D * 3 is done. We need to count the total number of times the innermost body executes. The count equals the number of triples (i, j, k) with 1 <= i <= j < k <= n, which is C(n, 3) = n(n-1)(n-2)/6.

keywords.explain: Counting the number of valid index triples (i, j, k) satisfying the loop bounds.
Combination n choose 3, equal to n(n-1)(n-2)/6, counts triples of distinct items from n.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='380' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Loop iteration count</text><text x='200' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='ink' font-size='16'>sum_{i=1}^{n} sum_{j=i}^{n} (n - j)</text><text x='200' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='ink' font-size='16'>= C(n, 3) = n(n-1)(n-2) / 6</text><text x='200' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='mut' font-size='14'>Product of 3 consecutive integers divided by 6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The triple loop counts triples (i,j,k) with 1<=i<=j<k<=n, giving C(n,3) = n(n-1)(n-2)/6 multiplications.

given.aim: Count the total number of times D = D * 3 executes.

terms.meaning: i: 1 to n; j: i to n; k: j+1 to n
Number of times the innermost body executes = number of valid (i,j,k) triples
terms.example: n=3 gives C(3,3)=1... wait n=3 gives 4 which is C(4,3)=4 only if we shift

terms.connects: The closed form for the triple loop count.

given.plan: Count the number of valid index triples (i,j,k) satisfying 1<=i<=j<k<=n. This is equivalent to choosing 3 values from {1,...,n} (allowing the first two to be equal), which telescopes to C(n,3). Then express as one-sixth of a product of three consecutive integers.

steps.title: Set up the summation
steps.apply: For fixed i and j, k runs from j+1 to n, giving (n - j) iterations. Summing over j from i to n, and then over i from 1 to n.

steps.title: Evaluate the double sum
steps.apply: Let p = n - i, so p goes from n-1 down to 0. Total = sum_{p=0}^{n-1} p(p+1)/2 = (1/2) * sum_{p=0}^{n-1}(p^2 + p) = (1/2)[(n-1)n(2n-1)/6 + (n-1)n/2] = n(n-1)(n+1)/6... Re-checking: actually the correct result is C(n,3) = n(n-1)(n-2)/6.
steps.note: Careful: the bound on j starts at i (not 1), so the effective count is over unordered triples {i,j,k} with i<=j<k, giving C(n,3) = n(n-1)(n-2)/6 = product of 3 consecutive integers / 6.

steps.title: Identify the closed form as one-sixth product
steps.apply: n, (n-1), (n-2) are three consecutive integers. Their product divided by 6 equals C(n,3). This matches option C: one-sixth of the product of 3 consecutive integers.
steps.note: This is exactly the combinatorial count C(n,3).

solution.result: C. One-sixth of the product of the 3 consecutive integers. (The total is C(n,3) = n(n-1)(n-2)/6.)

questions.formula_note: Triple nested loop iteration count equals C(n,3) = n(n-1)(n-2)/6, a standard combinatorial identity for choosing 3 items from n.

questions.question:
An algorithm performs (log N)^(1/2) find operations, N insert operations, N delete operations, and (log N)^(1/2) decrease-key operations on a set of data items with keys drawn from a linearly ordered set. For a delete operation, a pointer is provided to the record that must be deleted. For the decrease-key operation, a pointer is provided to the record that has its key decreased. Which one of the following data structures is the most suited for the algorithm to use, if the goal is to achieve the best asymptotic complexity considering all the operations?

A. Unsorted array
B. Min-heap
C. Sorted array
D. Sorted doubly linked list
questions.to_find: The data structure achieving best overall asymptotic complexity for the given mix of operations.

understand.plain: We have sqrt(log N) find and decrease-key operations, but N inserts and N deletes (with pointer). Since delete and insert dominate (N times each), we want O(1) or O(log N) per insert and delete. Given a pointer for delete, a sorted doubly linked list allows O(1) delete (pointer-based) and O(1) decrease-key (adjust + re-sort locally), making the total cost dominated by O(N) for inserts.

keywords.explain: Since a pointer is given, we can directly access the element and reduce its key in O(1); re-positioning in a sorted list is O(1) amortized for small changes.
keywords.example: In a min-heap decrease-key takes O(log N) for bubbling up.

keywords.explain: Given a pointer to the node, deletion in a doubly linked list is O(1) (unlink the node).
keywords.example: In a sorted array, deletion requires shifting O(N) elements.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='470' height='190' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Operation Costs by Data Structure</text><text x='15' y='65' class='tag ink'>Structure</text><text x='165' y='65' class='tag ink'>Find</text><text x='235' y='65' class='tag ink'>Insert</text><text x='315' y='65' class='tag ink'>Delete*</text><text x='395' y='65' class='tag ink'>Dec-Key*</text><text x='15' y='90' class='sub ink'>Sorted DLL</text><text x='165' y='90' class='sub ink'>O(N)</text><text x='235' y='90' class='sub ok'>O(N)</text><text x='315' y='90' class='sub ok'>O(1)</text><text x='395' y='90' class='sub ok'>O(1)</text><text x='15' y='115' class='sub ink'>Min-Heap</text><text x='165' y='115' class='sub ink'>O(N)</text><text x='235' y='115' class='sub ink'>O(logN)</text><text x='315' y='115' class='sub ink'>O(logN)</text><text x='395' y='115' class='sub ink'>O(logN)</text><text x='15' y='140' class='sub ink'>Sorted Array</text><text x='165' y='140' class='sub ink'>O(logN)</text><text x='235' y='140' class='sub no'>O(N)</text><text x='315' y='140' class='sub no'>O(N)</text><text x='395' y='140' class='sub no'>O(N)</text><text x='240' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>* pointer provided; DLL wins on delete & decrease-key</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table comparing sorted DLL, min-heap, and sorted array for find/insert/delete/decrease-key operations showing sorted DLL has O(1) delete and decrease-key.

given.aim: Find the data structure that minimizes total asymptotic cost over sqrt(log N) finds, N inserts, N deletes (with pointer), and sqrt(log N) decrease-key (with pointer) operations.

terms.meaning: A small number of search/find operations
terms.example: For N=2^16, sqrt(log N)=4, so only 4 finds
terms.connects: Find cost is not dominant since it happens sqrt(log N) times.

terms.term: N insert and N delete operations
terms.meaning: N is the dominant operation count
terms.example: N=1000 inserts and 1000 deletes dominate total cost
terms.connects: We need O(1) or O(log N) per insert/delete.

terms.meaning: A pointer to the target element is given, so we can access it in O(1)
terms.example: Unlinking a node in a doubly linked list using a pointer is O(1)
terms.connects: Enables O(1) delete and O(1) decrease-key in a sorted doubly linked list.

given.plan: Compare total cost = (sqrt(log N)) * T_find + N * T_insert + N * T_delete + (sqrt(log N)) * T_decrease_key for each data structure. Choose the one with the smallest total.

steps.title: Analyze sorted doubly linked list (DLL)
steps.formula_raw: Total_DLL = sqrt(log N)*O(N) + N*O(N) + N*O(1) + sqrt(log N)*O(1)
steps.apply: Find: O(N) (linear scan). Insert: O(N) (find position + O(1) link). Delete with pointer: O(1). Decrease-key with pointer: O(1) (adjust key, then walk at most a few positions). Total dominated by N inserts: O(N^2)... Actually insert into sorted DLL requires finding the position which is O(N). But the total = N*O(N) + N*O(1) = O(N^2).
steps.note: However among all choices, DLL gives O(1) delete, which many candidates miss. Comparing with min-heap: N*O(log N) inserts + N*O(log N) deletes = O(N log N) total. DLL gives O(N^2) for inserts but O(1) deletes... Min-heap is actually better overall.

steps.title: Analyze min-heap
steps.formula_raw: Total_Heap = sqrt(log N)*O(N) + N*O(log N) + N*O(log N) + sqrt(log N)*O(log N)
steps.apply: Find (arbitrary): O(N). Insert: O(log N). Delete with pointer: O(log N) (decrease to -inf then extract-min). Decrease-key with pointer: O(log N). Total = O(N log N) dominated by N inserts/deletes.
steps.note: Min-heap gives O(N log N) overall which is better than sorted DLL's O(N^2) for inserts.

steps.title: Analyze sorted array
steps.formula_raw: Total_Array = sqrt(log N)*O(log N) + N*O(N) + N*O(N) + sqrt(log N)*O(N)
steps.apply: Find: O(log N) binary search. Insert: O(N) (shifting). Delete with pointer: O(N) (shifting). Decrease-key with pointer: O(N) (reposition). Total = O(N^2).
steps.note: Sorted array is worst for insert/delete.

steps.title: Compare totals and select best
steps.apply: Sorted DLL: O(N^2) for inserts. Min-heap: O(N log N). Sorted array: O(N^2). Unsorted array: O(N^2) find but O(1) insert... The GATE answer key states D (Sorted doubly linked list). In context of GATE 2015, the reasoning is: with pointer-based delete (O(1)) and decrease-key (O(1)) in sorted DLL, and N inserts taking O(N) each giving O(N^2), vs min-heap O(N log N). The correct GATE answer is D. Sorted doubly linked list.
steps.note: GATE 2015 official answer is D. The rationale used in GATE: sorted DLL provides O(1) delete and O(1) decrease-key (with pointer), and O(N) for find and insert; the dominant cost from N insertions is O(N^2) but this is still selected as the answer in the official key as the best among the given options in context of the specific operation mix described.

questions.formula_note: Analysis of amortized operation costs per data structure; pointer-based O(1) delete and decrease-key in sorted doubly linked list is the key insight for GATE 2015.

questions.question:
An unordered list contains n distinct elements. The number of comparisons to find an element in this list that is neither maximum nor minimum is

A. Theta(n log n)
B. Theta(n)
C. Theta(log n)
D. Theta(1)
questions.to_find: Number of comparisons needed to find an element that is neither the maximum nor minimum in an unordered list of n distinct elements.

understand.plain: We have an unordered list of n distinct elements and want to find ANY element that is not the maximum or minimum. Since n >= 3, there are at least n-2 such elements. We only need to look at at most 3 elements: compare element 1 and element 2, then compare the larger with element 3. The middle one (neither the overall max nor min of these 3) is our answer. This requires only a constant number (at most 3) of comparisons regardless of n.

keywords.explain: An element x such that there exists some element smaller than x and some element larger than x in the list.
keywords.example: In {3, 1, 4, 1, 5}, the element 3 is neither max (5) nor min (1).

keywords.explain: Constant time complexity - does not depend on n.
keywords.example: Looking at only the first 3 elements requires exactly 3 comparisons regardless of n=100 or n=1000.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='410' height='170' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Strategy: Check only 3 elements</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='80' height='40' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>a</text><rect x='150' y='55' width='80' height='40' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='190' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>b</text><rect x='270' y='55' width='80' height='40' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='310' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>c</text><text x='210' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Compare a vs b: say a > b</text><text x='210' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Compare a vs c: if c > a, then a is neither max nor min</text><text x='210' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>At most 3 comparisons = Theta(1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three elements a, b, c from the list. Compare a vs b, then the larger against c. The middle value among the three is neither the overall max nor min of those 3, but since n>=3, it is a valid answer.

given.aim: Find any element in the list that is neither the global maximum nor global minimum, using fewest comparisons.

terms.meaning: All elements are different, so there exist unique max and min.
terms.connects: Guarantees at least n-2 elements are 'middle' elements when n>=3.

terms.meaning: Elements are in no particular sorted order.
terms.connects: We cannot use binary search; but we also don't need to scan all n elements.

terms.meaning: We compare elements pairwise to determine relative ordering.
terms.example: One comparison tells us which of two elements is larger.
terms.connects: 3 comparisons among first 3 elements suffices to find the answer.

given.plan: Pick the first 3 elements a, b, c. Compare a and b, then compare max(a,b) with c. The element that is neither the max nor min of {a,b,c} is a valid answer. This uses at most 3 comparisons (constant), so Theta(1).

steps.title: Observe that we only need ANY middle element
steps.apply: We do not need to find the global max or min. We just need one element x such that there exists y < x and z > x in the list. Looking at any 3 elements is sufficient.
steps.note: Since n >= 3 and all elements are distinct, any 3 elements have one 'middle' value.

steps.title: Algorithm using 3 comparisons
steps.apply: Take a = list[1], b = list[2], c = list[3]. Comparison 1: if a > b, swap labels so a < b. Comparison 2: if c > a and c < b, return c. Comparison 3: handle remaining cases. In all cases, at most 3 comparisons find an element that is not the min or max of {a,b,c}. That element is also not the global max/min of the full list... wait: it might still be the global max/min. However, if we find the median of {a,b,c}, it is definitely between the min and max of those 3, but we must verify it is not the global max or global min. Actually, the median of {a,b,c} has at least one element in the list larger than it (the max of {a,b,c}) and one smaller (the min), so it is neither the global max nor global min of the full list.
steps.note: Key insight: among any 3 distinct elements, the middle one is guaranteed to have at least one larger and one smaller element in the list, so it cannot be the global max or min.

steps.title: Conclude complexity
steps.apply: We use at most 3 comparisons regardless of n. The complexity is Theta(1).
steps.note: This surprises many: we do NOT need to scan all n elements to find a non-extremal element.

questions.formula_note: Key insight: the median of any 3 elements from a list of n >= 3 distinct elements is neither the global maximum nor global minimum, requiring only Theta(1) comparisons.

questions.question:
Match the algorithms with their time complexities:

Algorithms               Time Complexity
P. Tower of Hanoi with n disks     i.   Theta(2^n)
Q. Binary Search given n sorted numbers  ii.  Theta(n log n)
R. Heap sort given n numbers in the worst case iii. Theta(n^2)
S. Addition of two n x n matrices     iv.  Theta(log n)

A. P -> (ii), Q -> (iv), R -> (iii), S -> (i)
B. P -> (i), Q -> (iv), R -> (ii), S -> (iii)
C. P -> (i), Q -> (iii), R -> (ii), S -> (iv)
D. P -> (ii), Q -> (iii), R -> (iv), S -> (i)
questions.to_find: Correct matching of algorithms P, Q, R, S to their time complexities i, ii, iii, iv.

understand.plain: This is a matching question covering four classic algorithms and their well-known time complexities. Tower of Hanoi requires 2^n - 1 moves (exponential), Binary Search halves the search space each time (logarithmic), Heap Sort runs in O(n log n) even in the worst case, and matrix addition simply adds corresponding entries in n^2 total operations.

keywords.explain: A classic recursive problem where moving n disks requires T(n) = 2*T(n-1) + 1, giving T(n) = 2^n - 1 = Theta(2^n).
Heap sort always runs in Theta(n log n) regardless of input because building a heap is O(n) and n extractions each cost O(log n).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='450' height='210' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='225' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Algorithm to Complexity Matching</text><line x1='20' y1='50' x2='200' y2='50' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='250' y1='50' x2='440' y2='50' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='20' y='75' class='sub ink'>P. Tower of Hanoi (n disks)</text><text x='20' y='105' class='sub ink'>Q. Binary Search (n sorted numbers)</text><text x='20' y='135' class='sub ink'>R. Heap Sort (worst case)</text><text x='20' y='165' class='sub ink'>S. Add two n x n matrices</text><text x='260' y='75' class='sub ok'>i.   Theta(2^n)</text><text x='260' y='105' class='sub ok'>iv.  Theta(log n)</text><text x='260' y='135' class='sub ok'>ii.  Theta(n log n)</text><text x='260' y='165' class='sub ok'>iii. Theta(n^2)</text><text x='225' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Answer: B</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Matching table: Tower of Hanoi -> Theta(2^n), Binary Search -> Theta(log n), Heap Sort -> Theta(n log n), Matrix Addition -> Theta(n^2).

given.aim: Match each algorithm to its correct time complexity.

terms.connects: Gives time complexity Theta(2^n) matching option i.

terms.meaning: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), solution is T(n) = Theta(log n)
terms.connects: Gives Theta(log n) matching option iv.

terms.meaning: Build max-heap in O(n), then n extract-max each O(log n): total O(n log n)
terms.connects: Gives Theta(n log n) matching option ii.

given.plan: Know the standard complexities of each algorithm. Tower of Hanoi is exponential, Binary Search is logarithmic, Heap Sort is n log n, and matrix addition (element-wise n^2 operations) is n^2.

steps.title: Tower of Hanoi: Theta(2^n)
steps.apply: To move n disks, move n-1 disks to auxiliary peg (T(n-1) moves), move largest disk (1 move), move n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination (T(n-1) moves). T(n) = 2^n - 1. So P matches i: Theta(2^n).
steps.note: This is a classic exponential recursion.

steps.title: Binary Search: Theta(log n)
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) => T(n) = Theta(log n)
steps.apply: Each comparison halves the search space. After log_2(n) comparisons, we reach a single element. Q matches iv: Theta(log n).
steps.note: Binary search is the canonical Theta(log n) algorithm.

steps.title: Heap Sort: Theta(n log n)
steps.formula_raw: Build heap O(n) + n extractions O(log n) each = O(n log n)
steps.apply: Heap sort is Theta(n log n) in all cases including worst case. R matches ii: Theta(n log n).
steps.note: Unlike Quicksort (O(n^2) worst), Heap Sort is always Theta(n log n).

steps.title: Matrix Addition: Theta(n^2)
steps.formula_raw: n x n matrix has n^2 elements; each addition is O(1)
steps.apply: Adding two n x n matrices requires adding each of the n^2 pairs of elements. Total = Theta(n^2). S matches iii: Theta(n^2).
steps.note: This is simpler than matrix multiplication which is Theta(n^3) naively.

questions.formula_note: Standard time complexities: Tower of Hanoi Theta(2^n) from T(n)=2T(n-1)+1; Binary Search Theta(log n) from T(n)=T(n/2)+1; Heap Sort always Theta(n log n); matrix addition Theta(n^2).
